details.text: Derivative: انتَصِرْ the command form of اِنْتَصَرَ which means to avenge.
Derivative: مُّنْهَمِرٍ from اِنْهَمَرَ which means to pour down, severe and torrential rain that does not stop.
Derivative: أَلْوَاحٍ is the plural of لَوْح which is something with a flat surface and is used here to refer to boards and planks.

Derivative: مُدَّكِرٍ – ذَاكِر (Dhaakir) refers to someone who remembers. مُذْتَكِر (Mudhtakir) is someone who puts a lot of effort into remembering. The ذال (Dhaal - ذ) and تَاء (Taa’ – ت) are letters which are difficult to pronounce together as such مُذْتَكِر (Mudhtakir) has an easier to pronounce optional version of مُذَّكِر (Mudh’dhakir) and because it is an easier option it can imply a lessening of the verb in the case where the amount of effort related to remembering is less with مُذَّكِر (Mudh’dhakir) than مُذْتَكِر (Mudhtakir). And because مُذَّكِر (Mudh’dhakir) itself is harder to pronounce, a third change can be made making it easier to pronounce by exchanging the ذال (Dhaal - ذ) with a دال (Daal - د) thus producing مُدَّكِرٍ (Muddakir). As such, in the case where the original word مُذْتَكِر (Mudhtakir) means to put a lot of effort into remembering, مُدَّكِرٍ (Muddakir) implies putting the least amount of effort into trying to remember or take heed.
Derivative: صَرْصَرًا extremely cold, icy, relentless and violent, in reference to the wind (رِيحًا).
Derivative: نَحْسٍ means to be cursed, with no good at all in it, only misfortune.
Derivative: تَنْزِعُ from نَزَعَ which means to pluck, tear, strip or pull something out, like a plant from the ground.
Derivative: أَعْجَازُ refers to the stumps and roots of palm trees that are visible when they are uprooted.
Derivative: مُنْقَعِرٍ – to be hollowed out from قَعُرَ and اِنْقَعَرَ which means to hollow out or to make hollow in the inside of something.
Derivative: أَشِرٌ is used to describe someone who is wild, reckless and unstable and applies a rash arrogance or cockiness.
Derivative: مُحْتَضَرٌ means to be attended by each party. In can mean to attend something in alternation i.e., at the persons’ specified time.
Derivative: هَشِيمِ refers to the leftover twig fragments after some building with wood is undertaken.
Derivative: حَاصِبًا is a strong wind or storm.
Derivative: طَمَسْنَا from طَمَسَ which means to efface, obliterate or rub out. Here in reference to the eyes, it means to blind them and place a barrier over their eyes.
Derivative: مُسْتَقِرٌّ means something that is stable and here it means something that remains, abides and takes hold.
Derivative: مُقْتَدِرٍ (Muqtadir) means to possess great power and capability and is more emphatic than قَدِير (Qadeer) which is more emphatic than قَادِر (Qaadir).
Derivative: مُنْتَصِرٌ means the one who avenges and not the one whom vengeance is taken from and dominated i.e., those who dominate and are victorious.
Derivative: يُهْزَمُ from هَزَمَ means to put out of action, do away with, to defeat or put to flight.
Derivative: أَدْهَىٰ comes from the word دَاهِيَة (Daahiyah) which means a calamity or disaster. أَدْهَىٰ means even more disastrous or calamitous.

Derivative: يُسْحَبُونَ from the verb سَحَبَ which means to hold or keep something down low (unusual translation from the Ustadh – The following is the usual understanding). It means to be dragged along or to trail something upon the ground.
Derivative: لَمْحٍ means a glance, quick look or instant (of time).
- Literally means: “Like the glance of the sight”. It is similar to the expression “in the blink of an eye” and is an expression to mean something that is instant or extremely quick.

Derivative: أَشْيَاعَ means those people or nations which are similar and fall under the same category or brand as each other. With كُم (your) here in the statement أَشْيَاعَكُمْ (Your أَشْيَاعَ) it means your kinds or your likes.
Derivative: مُّسْتَطَرٌ means to be documented and recorded, line by line.
Derivative: مَقْعَدِ (Maq’ad) is a place that you remain sitting in for a long time as opposed to مَجْلِس (Majlis) which is just a place that you sit in.
Derivative: صِدْقٍ means “of truth” and is a description of the place of sitting for the Muttaqeen in Jannah. It is a description of praise and honour for such a place to sit.

Derivative: نُكُرٍ refers to something that is unrecognised, that has not been seen before and is disliked. The scholars of Tafseer say that this refers to a huge and terrible affair which people inwardly are unprepared for and unaccustomed to, on the Day of Judgement when the trumpet is blown.
Derivative: خُشَّعًا filled with horror and exhaustion. (Strange translation and contrary to what I have read from the Mufassireen).
Derivative: الْأَجْدَاثِ means graves and can refer to those which no longer have any markings and nobody even knew that they were there.
Derivative: مُّهْطِعِينَ from أْهْطَعَ which means to walk or run fast whilst keeping your eyes directed towards a target.
